title: Quantum Matter Makes Lightcones Quantum
abstract: In gravitational physics, matter does not merely move within spacetime; it also determines the light cones that define causal relations. What happens when the matter that determines these light cones is itself in a quantum state? We address this question in a controlled low-energy setting: a massless scalar field propagating in the spacetime with the Newtonian gravitational potential sourced by a non-relativistic quantum particle. We show that the light cones are affected by an operator-valued Shapiro delay, with the three consequences: (i) causal-boundary shifts are promoted to noncommuting observables, giving the causal structure an irreducible quantum uncertainty; (ii) the causal relation between two fixed spacetime points can become a superposition of timelike and spacelike configurations; and (iii) tracing out the source smears the Wightman light-cone singularity, producing an effective UV cutoff. Thus, quantum matter does not merely fluctuate within spacetime; it makes the causal structure itself quantum, even without quantized gravitons.
summary: This theoretical paper explores the quantum nature of spacetime causality by examining a scalar field propagating near a quantum source. It demonstrates that the source's quantum fluctuations induce an operator-valued shift in the light cones, making the causal structure inherently uncertain. This effect is calculated perturbatively and shows that quantum matter fundamentally alters the geometry of spacetime correlations.
main_problem: The paper investigates how the causal structure of spacetime, defined by light cones, becomes quantum when the matter source generating the gravitational field is itself quantum mechanical.
main_result: Quantum matter makes the causal structure itself quantum, leading to non-commuting causal-boundary shifts and smearing the light-cone singularity, even without quantizing gravity.
method: The analysis uses perturbative field-theoretic calculations in the weak-field limit, focusing on the operator-valued Shapiro time delay ($\hat{	au}_n$) for a massless scalar field.
model_or_system: The system models a massless scalar field propagating in a spacetime perturbed by a weak Newtonian gravitational potential sourced by a non-relativistic quantum particle.
key_observables: Operator-valued Shapiro delay ($\hat{	au}_n$), noncommuting causal-boundary shifts, and the smearing of the Wightman light-cone singularity.
important_parameters: Low-energy, non-relativistic limit; the gravitational coupling strength (related to $GM/b$); Planck scales ($\ell_{Pl}, t_{Pl}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a weak-field approximation and uses an instantaneous Newtonian potential, avoiding full quantum gravity treatment.
figures_summary: Figure 1 schematically shows the effect of the weak potential on null rays. Figure 2 illustrates the quantum Shapiro delay ($\hat{	au}_n$) shift. Figure 4 shows the superposition of causal relations when the source is in a spatial superposition.
paper_structure: The paper develops the problem by first establishing the classical Shapiro delay, then quantizing this delay into an operator ($\hat{	au}_n$). It analyzes the consequences of this operator on the commutator and Wightman function, demonstrating non-commutativity and smearing effects.
why_interesting: While focused on gravity, the core mechanism—quantifying how quantum uncertainty in a source modifies fundamental geometric/causal structures—is highly relevant to open quantum systems where environmental coupling dictates observable dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic configuration of the unperturbed light cone (black cone) and the source- particle trajectory (blue line). We study how null rays (orange lines) passing near the quantum- mechanical source (blue dot) are affected by its weak Newtonian gravitational potential. The impact parameter b is the closest spatial separation between an unperturbed ray and the source trajectory. The Shapiro time delay is not yet reflected in this picture.

Figure 2. Schematic picture of the quantum Shapiro delay ˆτn defined in Eq. (3.4). The blue dot represents the quantum source particle at x = ˆqI(t). The weak Newtonian potential sourced by this particle shifts the original Minkowski light cone (gray diagonal line). The orange solid line shows the mean delayed light cone, shifted by ¯τn = ⟨ˆτn⟩, while the fluctuation δˆτn = ˆτn −¯τn makes its position quantum-mechanically uncertain, as indicated by the purple dashed lines. This is a 2D projection of the 3+1-dimensional geometry; the ray passes near the source without intersecting it (see Fig. 1).

Figure 3. Two null rays emitted from the spatial origin x = 0 at times Ta and Tb in the directions na and nb, respectively. Their quantum Shapiro-delay fluctuations, δˆτa and δˆτb, depend on the position fluctuations of the same source particle evaluated at different times, δ ˆqI(sa) and δ ˆqI(sb), where sa and sb denote the respective closest-approach times. Since source-position operators at different times do not commute in general, the two causal-boundary shifts also do not commute with each other in general, [δˆτa, δˆτb] ̸= 0.

Figure 4. Schematic picture of the superposition of causal relations induced by a single delocalized particle, namely, a single source particle in a spatial superposition. For simplicity, the source wave packets are assumed to be sharply localized in the two branches, and their intrinsic widths are neglected. In the |N⟩branch (left blue dot and upper orange line) the source is closer to the ray and produces a larger Shapiro delay than in the |F⟩branch (right blue dot and lower dark orange line). The resulting branch-dependent causal boundaries (two orange lines) divide spacetime into three regions.
